items.question: Who developed the modern soft-margin support vector machine?
items.answer: Corinna Cortes and Vladimir Vapnik published the soft-margin SVM in 1995, building on Vapnik's earlier statistical learning theory and the 1992 hard-margin formulation with Boser and Guyon.

items.question: What is a support vector in an SVM?
items.answer: A training example with non-zero dual weight αᵢ that lies on the margin boundary (yᵢ(wᵀxᵢ + b) = 1 in hard margin) or inside/violating the margin in soft margin — these points alone determine the hyperplane.

items.question: When should you use soft-margin SVM instead of hard margin?
items.answer: When classes overlap, labels are noisy, or data is not perfectly linearly separable — soft margin with tuned C allows slack ξᵢ so optimization remains feasible and generalizes better than forcing zero violations.

items.question: Where does the geometric margin appear in the SVM objective?
items.answer: Maximizing geometric margin 2/||w||₂ is equivalent to minimizing ||w||₂² subject to yᵢ(wᵀxᵢ + b) ≥ 1; geometric margin for one point is yᵢ(wᵀxᵢ + b) / ||w||₂ — distance to hyperplane in feature space.

items.question: Why does SVM focus on maximizing margin rather than minimizing training error alone?
items.answer: Structural risk minimization: a wider margin on training data tends to bound generalization error via VC theory — the boundary is chosen for robust separation, not just fitting labels, which helps on unseen points.

items.question: How do you classify a new point with a trained linear SVM?
items.answer: Compute f(x) = wᵀx + b (decision_function in sklearn). Predict class +1 if f(x) ≥ 0, else −1. Only support vectors contribute if using the dual sum f(x) = Σ αᵢ yᵢ K(xᵢ, x) + b.

items.question: SVM vs logistic regression — objective and output?
items.answer: SVM: maximize margin with hinge slack, output sign(wᵀx + b) score. Logistic regression: minimize BCE, output σ(wᵀx + b) probability. Both can learn linear boundaries; SVM gives sparse support vectors, logistic regression gives calibrated probabilities.

items.question: SVM vs k-NN — memory and inference?
items.answer: SVM stores support vectors only (subset of training) plus w, b; prediction is O(#support vectors). k-NN stores all training points; prediction scans neighbors — O(n) per query. SVM learns a global boundary; k-NN is local voting.

items.question: Large C vs small C in soft-margin SVM?
items.answer: Large C: heavy slack penalty → narrower margin, fewer training violations, risk overfitting noise. Small C: light slack penalty → wider margin, more tolerated violations, smoother boundary — often better when classes overlap.

items.question: What if you remove a non-support-vector training point after fitting?
items.answer: Predictions and the hyperplane w, b stay identical — non-support vectors have αᵢ = 0 and do not appear in the dual sum. Retrain only if you removed a support vector.

items.question: What if C is set extremely large on noisy overlapping data?
items.answer: The optimizer minimizes slack almost at all costs → ||w|| grows, margin narrows, boundary wiggles to classify every training point including outliers → high training accuracy but poor test generalization.

items.question: Cause: Features are standardized before LinearSVC. Effect?
items.answer: All dimensions contribute on comparable scale to ||w|| and margin geometry; C behaves consistently across features and solvers converge more reliably than on raw mixed-unit inputs.

items.question: Cause: One mislabeled point lies deep inside the opposite class (soft margin, moderate C). Effect?
items.answer: That point often becomes a support vector with active slack; the boundary shifts to balance margin width against the C-weighted penalty of misclassifying it — may reduce margin and hurt generalization if C is too large.
